expertise: NLP and automatic-evaluation / LLM-as-a-judge methodology (meta-evaluation, agreement metrics, alt-test, jury/panel methods, SummEval/G-Eval replication, annotator disagreement / perspectivism, contamination probing).
summary: The paper offers a four-study program asking when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing (HCC). Study 1a is a PRISMA review of 217 cross-domain studies that maps reliability against the human-human ceiling (mean 3.15/5; 31% in the reliable band). Study 1b re-runs the G-Eval/SummEval summary-level protocol with four frontier open models and reports they now match/exceed the reported GPT-4 G-Eval correlations on consistency/coherence but not fluency. Study 2 is a PRISMA review of 55 HCC/HCI/qualitative studies coding method x role (augment/replace/simulate) x epistemic stance, finding an augment-not-replace consensus. Study 3 runs a 6-model open panel (9B-754B) across 6 datasets (ChaosNLI-SNLI/MNLI, GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain, MultiPICo) for 18,000 judgments, benchmarking against the human ceiling with accuracy/kappa/macro-F1, a count-based alt-test, a majority jury, a verbatim-recall contamination probe, and a mixed-effects analysis. The organizing claim is that judge reliability is bounded by the human-agreement ceiling and governed by item-level disagreement rather than model scale or contamination. The synthesis is a three-regime decision framework (automate / augment / keep humans). The work is unusually self-critical about its own limitations (single-reviewer LLM-assisted coding, a contamination probe that false-positives, a non-robust dataset-level subjectivity coefficient) and releases code and corpora. The central weakness from an NLP-evaluation standpoint is that the empirical contributions (1b and 3) are narrow and rest on several methodological choices that are either non-standard, unverifiable from the text, or in tension with the paper's own framing, and the headline "frontier open models now match proprietary judges" claim compares fresh runs against numbers copied from a 2023 paper rather than a re-run baseline.
strengths: The reframing - that an LLM judge cannot exceed the human-human agreement ceiling, and that reliability is governed by item-level disagreement rather than model scale - is a genuinely useful organizing principle and is well-aligned with the learning-with-disagreement / perspectivism literature (Plank, Aroyo & Welty, Nie et al. ChaosNLI). Operationalizing it on perspectivist corpora (MultiPICo, ChaosNLI) is a sensible and timely move.

Methodologically careful in places that most LLM-judge papers ignore: anchoring to a random-annotator-vs-majority ceiling instead of a single 'gold', using a count-based alt-test to gate replacement claims, reporting macro-F1 and kappa alongside accuracy on imbalanced tasks, and stratifying accuracy by item entropy (acc-low vs acc-high disagreement). The disagreement-aware evaluation guidance in 7.4 is the kind of checklist the field needs.

Commendable scientific honesty: the paper retracts its own earlier dataset-level 'subjectivity' coefficient when it fails leave-one-dataset-out and cluster-robust SEs, and it openly reports that its contamination probe false-positives on a guaranteed-clean control and therefore declines to lean on it. This is rare and raises trust in the remaining claims.

Reproducibility posture is strong: temperature 0, documented model identifiers, released harnesses, auto-generated statistics macros, power justification at n=500, and 100% parse rate. The two coded corpora are released for re-coding.

The augment/replace/simulate x stance coding in Study 2 surfaces a clear and decision-relevant pattern (36 augment vs 14 replace vs 5 simulate; reliability augment > replace > simulate) that is directly useful to HCC practitioners and is positioned as the first HCC-specific synthesis of this literature.
weaknesses: The Study 1b headline ('the most powerful open models now match or exceed proprietary judges on SummEval') is built on an unfair comparison. The open models are run fresh, but the G-Eval bar is the *number reported in the 2023 paper*, not a re-run GPT-4/G-Eval baseline under the identical harness. Differences in parsing, the JSON-only single-score output (which discards G-Eval's probability-weighted/form-filling scoring), prompt wording, and SummEval preprocessing can easily move summary-level Spearman by more than the ~0.02 margin claimed (deepseek 0.542 vs 0.523). Without re-running G-Eval/GPT-4 in the same pipeline, the 'beats proprietary' claim is not supported.

The summary-level Spearman comparison is statistically fragile and reported without uncertainty. Summary-level rho averages per-document correlations over only 16 summaries per document; the per-aspect averages (0.50-0.54 vs 0.52) have no confidence intervals or significance test, so '3 of 4 beat the bar' could be noise. The paper itself notes a noiseless oracle scores only ~0.87 on fluency, underscoring how compressed and unstable these correlations are.

Study 3's empirical scope is too thin to carry the framework's weight. Six datasets, all subjective *classification* (NLI, sentiment, offensiveness, hate, irony), with most of the conceptual payload (qualitative coding, participant simulation, design judgment) resting entirely on the Study 2 review. The decision framework's 'augment' and 'keep humans' regimes are therefore review-grounded assertions dressed in the authority of an 18,000-judgment experiment. The number 18,000 is inflated relative to its evidentiary content (6 models x 6 datasets x 500 items).

The mixed-effects analysis has too few level-2 units to support its conclusions. With 6 datasets, the random-intercept SDs for dataset (0.73) vs item (2.02) are being compared across wildly different unit counts (6 vs 3,000), so 'item variance dominates' is partly mechanical; high-entropy items are near-definitionally harder to predict, making (ii) somewhat circular. The GLMM/GLM specification (Bayesian priors, cluster definition, why base-rate skew enters as a single OR=8.2 predictor) is under-described in Appendix C, and OR=8.2 for base-rate skew is suspiciously large and likely a coding/scaling artifact that needs explanation.

The alt-test application is questionable on the high-annotator-count NLI sets. ChaosNLI has ~100 annotators/item; a count-based leave-one-annotator-out winning rate against ~100 humans is a very different (and much easier/structurally different) test than on the 3-annotator social sets. Reporting a single 0.5 threshold across datasets with 3 vs ~100 annotators conflates incomparable regimes, and the paper does not discuss how annotator count interacts with alt-test power or interpretation.

Construct-validity problems in Study 3 datasets undercut the 'subjective social computing' narrative. GoEmotions is natively 27 fine-grained emotions; the paper collapses it to a 4-way positive/negative/ambiguous/neutral 'sentiment' scheme that is the authors' own mapping, not GoEmotions' ceiling. The reported 0.78 ceiling and the judge prompt ('overall sentiment') therefore measure an artifact of the remapping, not GoEmotions as released. This is exactly the granularity/base-rate confound the paper warns about, here introduced by the authors.

Both systematic reviews are single-reviewer and LLM-assisted, including the screening, extraction, AND the 'independent' re-coding used to validate them. The alpha = 0.87 is therefore rubric-reproducibility of one automated pipeline against another, not inter-rater reliability, as the paper concedes - which means the entire 217+55 corpus, the reliability scores, and every domain/method aggregate inherit the systematic biases of a single uncalibrated LLM coder with no human ground-truth anchor. For a paper whose thesis is 'do not trust LLM judgment on subjective coding without human validation,' using an unvalidated LLM judge to produce the review's core subjective codes is a self-undermining design.

Model identifiers are non-verifiable and appear to be future/placeholder versions (deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5.1, qwen3.6, gemma-4-31b, gptoss-120b). These do not correspond to publicly documented models as of the stated mid-2026 scope, are served via an unnamed 'institutional vLLM API,' and cannot be reproduced by external reviewers. The 'most powerful open models available today' claim is unsubstantiated and the catalogue/selection criterion is undocumented.

Several quantitative inconsistencies between text, tables, and figures erode confidence in the auto-generated-stats claim. E.g., abstract/Table 4 vs Figure 12 disagree on best-model values (ChaosNLI-SNLI best 0.84 in Table 4 but 0.86 jury; HateXplain 'best 0.66' text vs 0.66 gemma but figure cells show 0.66/0.63); SBIC ceiling appears as 0.905 in text and Table 5 but 0.91 in Table 4/6; the human ceiling for ChaosNLI-SNLI is 0.75 in Table 4 but 0.752 elsewhere. Minor individually, but they contradict the 'manuscript cannot drift from the data' claim.

Reference [30] (James et al. 2026, 'Counting on Consensus', arXiv:2603.06865) cites a non-existent future arXiv ID (26xx month), and several Study 3 'frontier' models post-date any verifiable release, raising concern that parts of the evidence base are fabricated or simulated rather than real runs. Given the paper's own admission that its contamination probe confabulates, reviewers cannot rule out that some reported numbers are themselves model-generated rather than measured.
required_revisions: Re-run the G-Eval/GPT-4 (or a clearly named current proprietary judge) baseline inside the exact Study 1b harness (same prompt, same JSON parsing, same SummEval preprocessing, same summary-level rho computation) and compare open vs proprietary head-to-head. Do not compare fresh runs to numbers copied from the 2023 paper. Report bootstrap CIs and a paired significance test on the per-document correlation differences.

Add uncertainty quantification to every comparison currently asserted as 'beats/matches': bootstrap CIs and paired tests for Study 1b per-aspect Spearman, and CIs/tests for all Study 3 'best model vs ceiling' and jury-vs-ceiling comparisons (not only the BH ceiling-gap panel).

Document the exact model identifiers, providers, parameter counts, quantization, and release dates for every judge, and state the explicit selection criterion for 'most powerful open models.' If these are internal/renamed models, map them to public checkpoints or the results are not reproducible and the 'frontier open' claim must be removed.

Provide genuine human inter-rater reliability for both review corpora: have at least two trained human coders independently re-code a substantial random sample (>=20%) of the reliability verdict and role/stance codes, and report human-human Krippendorff alpha. The current LLM-vs-LLM alpha=0.87 cannot validate an LLM-produced subjective coding scheme; this is essential given the paper's own thesis.

Justify or replace the GoEmotions 4-way 'sentiment' remapping. Either evaluate GoEmotions on its native label set (with its native ceiling) or clearly relabel this as an authors-defined sentiment task and recompute the ceiling and base-rate from the original annotator distributions, with the mapping released and audited.

Fully specify the inferential models in the appendix: the GLMM likelihood/priors/software, the exact definition and scaling of 'base-rate skew' (explain the OR=8.2), the granularity coding, the cluster definition for robust SEs, convergence diagnostics, and a sensitivity analysis showing the item-vs-dataset variance comparison is not an artifact of unit counts.

Reconcile every numeric discrepancy across abstract, Tables 2/4/5/6, and Figures 5/11/12 (ceilings, best-model accuracies, jury values). If statistics are truly auto-generated, regenerate all tables/figures from one source and verify.

Clarify and justify the alt-test across heterogeneous annotator counts: report annotators/item per dataset, discuss how the count-based winning rate and the 0.5 threshold are interpretable when A ranges from 3 to ~100, and consider a per-item-normalized or power-aware variant.

Verify reference [30] and any other future-dated citations; correct or remove invalid arXiv IDs. Provide a data-availability statement that lets a reviewer confirm the Study 1b/Study 3 runs are real (e.g., raw per-item judge outputs, even if only labels are public).
requested_experiments: Head-to-head proprietary baseline in the same harness: run GPT-4o / Claude / a named current proprietary model AND the open panel on SummEval (all 1600 summaries x 4 aspects) using the identical Study 1b prompt and parser; report summary-level Spearman per aspect with 1000-sample bootstrap CIs and a paired Wilcoxon test on per-document correlations. Also run the original G-Eval probability-weighted scoring variant to isolate how much of the gap is the scoring method vs the model.

Prompt-and-parsing robustness for Study 1b: re-run SummEval with (a) the verbatim G-Eval chain-of-thought form-filling prompt and (b) the JSON-single-score prompt for the same models, to quantify how much the reported correlations depend on the simplified output format.

GoEmotions native-label experiment: re-run the 6-model panel on GoEmotions with its original 27-label (or 7-cluster Ekman) scheme, compute the native human ceiling and per-class macro-F1, and report whether the 'subjective social construct' shortfall persists under the dataset's own taxonomy rather than the authors' 4-way mapping.

Expand Study 3 toward the regimes the framework actually claims: add at least one public multi-coder qualitative-coding dataset (e.g., a released codebook-coded corpus with >=2 human coders) and one perspectivist toxicity set with annotator demographics beyond MultiPICo, so that the 'augment' boundary is experiment-grounded rather than review-asserted.

Alt-test sensitivity to annotator count: subsample ChaosNLI items to 3, 5, and 10 annotators and recompute the alt-test winning rate, to show whether the NLI 'pass' is an artifact of the ~100-annotator regime rather than genuine substitutability.

Soft-label / distribution-matching evaluation: report cross-entropy or Jensen-Shannon between each model's predicted label distribution (via repeated sampling or token probabilities) and the human soft-label distribution on ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, and MultiPICo, since the paper's own thesis is that forced-majority accuracy is the wrong target for contested constructs.

Leave-one-dataset-out and added-dataset stability for the mixed-effects conclusions: re-fit the GLMM after adding 3-4 more datasets and report whether the item-level-dominance and base-rate-skew effects hold, addressing the n=6 level-2 fragility.
minor_comments: The phrase 'most powerful open models available today' recurs but is never operationalized; give the catalogue snapshot date and ranking criterion.

Figure 5 appears twice (Study 1b SummEval bars and 'Mean LLM-judge reliability by domain' text); the figure numbering and embedded sub-figure labels (Figure 6, 11, 15, S2-x) are inconsistent with the in-text Figure 1-13 numbering and are confusing.

The contamination probe section is honest but ultimately contributes little; consider moving it fully to the appendix and replacing the Table 4 'Contam. tier' column (uniformly MEDIUM, admittedly an artifact) with the more informative low-recall-model signal.

State whether decoding at temperature 0 with a single sample is appropriate for the jury, since a temperature-0 'panel' of deterministic models is not a diversity-of-opinion jury in the Verga et al. sense; clarify what diversity the jury actually provides.
The claim 'bigger is not always better: 31B gemma ties the 754B glm' is interesting but rests on point estimates without CIs; temper or test it.
Define 'reliability score = 6 - verdict' consistently; the dual scale (1=validated verdict vs 5=validated score) is a frequent source of reader confusion across tables.
Clarify the n for the alt-test denominators and how ties in the leave-one-annotator-out comparison are handled.

The ethics discussion of encoding majority bias via the majority-label ceiling is thoughtful; consider reporting results against soft labels as the primary metric, not only as guidance to others, to practice what the paper preaches.

area: HCI / CSCW qualitative methods and research epistemology
expertise: HCI/CSCW qualitative methods and research epistemology; systematic-review methodology (PRISMA); inter-coder reliability; LLM-as-a-judge / NLP evaluation
summary: The paper presents four studies aimed at telling Human-Centered Computing (HCC) researchers when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for, assist, or must not replace human judgment. Study 1a is a PRISMA review of 217 cross-domain studies producing a "green/amber/red" reliability landscape anchored to a human-human ceiling. Study 1b re-runs the G-Eval/SummEval summarization protocol with four contemporary open models to test whether scale moves the landscape (a "matter of time" vs. "structural ceiling" distinction). Study 2 is a PRISMA review of 55 HCC/HCI/qualitative-methods studies coded by method, LLM role (augment/replace/simulate), and epistemic stance, finding an augment-not-replace consensus under a dominant stance of "tension." Study 3 is an empirical 6-model, 6-dataset (18,000-judgment) meta-evaluation on social-computing/NLI datasets with human labels, benchmarked against the human ceiling, with an alt-test, a jury, a contamination probe, and a mixed-effects analysis localizing reliability to item-level human disagreement rather than dataset-level subjectivity. The unifying claim is that an LLM judge can only be as reliable as the human task's verifiable agreement ceiling, and scaling does not raise that ceiling. The paper is admirably transparent about its own limitations and releases code and corpora. However, its central methodological foundation (two large reviews) rests on a single-coder, LLM-assisted pipeline whose reliability validation is itself LLM-on-LLM, and the strongest interpretive HCC claims (the parts most relevant to a CHI audience) are review-grounded rather than empirically tested.
strengths: Genuinely useful and timely synthesis for HCC: the augment/replace/simulate x method x stance coding and the resulting decision framework address a real, unsettled question the community faces, and the framing distinction between a temporal capability gap and a structural human-ceiling limit is a clear, transferable contribution.

The human-ceiling anchoring is the right epistemological move and is well grounded in the relevant HCI/CSCW and NLP literature (McDonald, Schoenebeck & Forte on IRR as negotiated practice; Aroyo & Welty; Plank on human label variation; perspectivist corpora). Study 3 operationalizes 'disagreement as signal' concretely (entropy-stratified accuracy, soft-label JS divergence, alt-test), which is methodologically more honest than the usual accuracy-vs-gold reporting.

Unusual and commendable scientific candor: the authors actively undercut their own contamination probe with a clean post-cutoff control, retract the dataset-level 'subjectivity' coefficient after a leave-one-out robustness check, mark conjectures vs. data-grounded claims in the critical-assessment section, and explicitly scope which deployment regimes are experiment-grounded vs. review-grounded.

Strong reproducibility posture: PRISMA flow diagrams, released search logs, two coded corpora, judging prompts, power justification, auto-generated statistics macros, and a make-all pipeline. Ethics handling of HateXplain/SBIC (labels/aggregates only, no raw text) is appropriate.

The mixed-effects localization of variance to the item level (random-intercept SD 2.02 across items vs 0.73 datasets, 0.086 models) is a substantive, defensible finding that reframes 'subjectivity' as item-level human disagreement and supports the paper's organizing principle.
weaknesses: The methodological backbone of the paper (Studies 1a and 2) is a single-coder, LLM-assisted extraction and coding pipeline, and the reliability check uses LLM-assisted re-coders. By the authors' own admission this measures 'rubric reproducibility,' not human inter-rater agreement. For a paper whose central epistemological argument is that human interpretive judgment is not substitutable by LLMs, using an LLM pipeline to produce the interpretive verdicts that drive Studies 1-2 is a deep, self-undermining tension that the Krippendorff alpha=0.87 does not resolve (it is LLM-vs-LLM agreement, which can be high precisely because of shared model priors/biases). This is the single largest threat to validity.

There is a gap between the paper's headline scope and what is actually tested. The CHI-relevant claims (qualitative interpretive coding, participant simulation, persona generation, expert design judgment are 'unsafe to substitute') rest entirely on the Study 2 review of secondary literature, not on any new evidence. Study 3 tests only short-text subjective classification (NLI, sentiment, offensiveness, hate, irony) which is closer to crowd annotation than to interpretive qualitative analysis. The title 'When Can a Machine Judge Like a Human?' and the HCC framing over-promise relative to the empirical contribution.

Several quantitative claims rest on very small n cells that the paper acknowledges but still uses rhetorically. Study 1a domain means include n=1-3 cells (e.g., Argument Quality n=1, MT n=3); Study 2 method means include n=1-2 cells (Creativity/ideation n=1, several at n=2). Krippendorff alpha for Study 2 is computed on n=16, and the stance code (alpha=0.59) is only moderately reliable yet 'tension dominates' (38/55) is a prominent narrative claim. The reliability rubric (a 5-level ordinal verdict) is itself a subjective construct; collapsing heterogeneous metrics (kappa, alpha, Spearman, accuracy, ICC, percent agreement) into one ordinal 'verdict' risks incommensurability the within-family caveat does not fully address.

The 'human ceiling' definition (random-annotator-vs-majority accuracy) is itself contestable and arguably stacks the comparison. For datasets with ~100 annotators (ChaosNLI) vs ~3 (HateXplain, SBIC), the random-annotator-vs-majority statistic has very different meaning and stability; comparing an LLM panel to this ceiling across such different annotator regimes may not be apples-to-apples, and the choice of majority-as-target is the very majority-flattening the paper critiques (acknowledged in Ethics, but it still drives the verdicts).

Study 1b is thin as a 'moving target' test: a single benchmark (SummEval), integer-only scoring (the authors note probability-weighted scoring was not run, which materially affects the fluency claim), and a comparison against reported G-Eval numbers rather than a re-run of G-Eval/GPT-4 under identical conditions. The central time-vs-structure dichotomy is interesting but is supported by essentially one aspect (fluency) of one dataset.

Related-work positioning to qualitative HCI is somewhat thin given the framing. The reflexive-TA / interpretivist critique is invoked (Braun & Clarke; Schroeder et al.; Davison et al.) but the paper does not seriously engage what 'reliability' even means for interpretivist work, where IRR is contested as a positivist import. Treating qualitative coding agreement on a kappa/alpha scale, then concluding LLMs are 'reliable as augmentation,' partly reinscribes the positivist measurement frame that the cited qualitative scholars resist, without acknowledging that tension.
required_revisions: Provide at least one genuine human inter-coder reliability check for both review corpora: have two or more independent human experts (not LLM-assisted) re-code the held-out samples on the verdict, role, and stance codes, and report human Krippendorff alpha alongside the existing LLM-rubric reproducibility. If human IRR is materially lower, temper Studies 1-2 claims accordingly. The current LLM-on-LLM alpha cannot stand as the reliability guarantee for an interpretive coding pipeline in a paper arguing LLMs cannot do interpretation.

Re-scope the title, abstract, and contribution claims to match evidence. Make explicit (in the abstract, not only in section 7.5) that the interpretive-qualitative, participant-simulation, persona, and design-judgment regimes are review-grounded conjecture, and that Study 3's new empirical evidence covers only subjective short-text classification/annotation. Avoid implying the paper empirically establishes the 'keep humans' regime.

Engage the epistemological critique of IRR/agreement for interpretivist qualitative research head-on. Add a paragraph (citing McDonald et al. and the reflexive-TA literature) clarifying that for interpretivist work, agreement is not the relevant validity criterion, and explain why a reliability-verdict framing is nonetheless defensible (or restrict the verdict framing to post-positivist/coding-reliability traditions).

Report sensitivity of all aggregate claims to small-n cells. Re-present Study 1a and Study 2 figures/tables with a minimum-n threshold (e.g., suppress or grey out n<3 means) and confirm that the green/amber/red ordering and the augment>replace>simulate gradient survive. Recompute the augment/replace/simulate reliability gap with confidence intervals or a nonparametric test rather than bare means (3.17 vs 3.07 vs 2.80).

Strengthen Study 1b before it can support the time-vs-structure claim: re-run the actual G-Eval/GPT-4 protocol under the same harness (not just compare to reported numbers), add probability-weighted/expected-value scoring as the authors concede is needed for fluency, and add at least one more promising-band benchmark so the structural-ceiling argument does not rest on a single aspect of a single dataset.

Justify and stress-test the human-ceiling estimator. Show robustness of every verdict to alternative ceiling definitions (pairwise agreement, leave-one-annotator-out, soft-label cross-entropy) and explicitly analyze how the differing annotator counts (~3 vs ~100) affect ceiling stability and the LLM-vs-ceiling comparison.

Clarify the LLM-assisted SEARCH/SCREENING pipeline's recall and bias. Report how many of the 217+55 included studies were found by LLM-deep-search vs OpenAlex/Semantic Scholar, and provide a recall check (e.g., a known seed set of HCI/CSCW papers the search should have caught) so readers can assess whether the corpus is representative of HCC rather than skewed toward NLP/CSS venues (CSCW n=2, UIST/IUI absent from the corpus tables despite being claimed in the sweep).
requested_experiments: Human IRR study: recruit 2-3 independent human coders (with qualitative-methods training) to re-code the 25%/30% held-out samples of the Study 1a (n=54) and Study 2 (n=16) corpora on the 5-level verdict, the augment/replace/simulate role, and the epistemic stance, blind to the primary codes; report ordinal Krippendorff alpha per code and compare directly to the LLM-rubric alpha. Report disagreement patterns (which verdict bands and which stances humans disagree on).

Study 1b extension: re-run G-Eval with GPT-4 (or the closest reproducible proprietary judge) inside the paper's own SummEval harness, add expected-value (probability-weighted) scoring for all four open models, and add a second promising-band benchmark (e.g., TopicalChat/USR for dialogue or a RAG faithfulness set) to test whether the verifiable-vs-compressed-aspect pattern generalizes beyond SummEval fluency.

Ceiling-robustness analysis on Study 3: recompute all per-cell verdicts under three ceiling estimators (random-annotator-vs-majority [current], mean pairwise human agreement, and soft-label cross-entropy/KL against the full human distribution) and report how many of the 36 judge x dataset verdicts change band. This directly tests whether the headline 'ceiling governs reliability' result is an artifact of the chosen estimator.

A small qualitative-coding pilot to partially ground the 'augment-only' interpretive regime with new evidence: take one or two public datasets that DO carry multi-coder qualitative codes (the authors note these are scarce but exist, e.g., released CSCW/CHI coded transcripts or the CHALET/CollabCoder artifacts) and run the same panel+alt-test+disagreement pipeline, so at least one interpretive-coding data point is experiment-grounded rather than review-only.

Prompt-paraphrase robustness expanded: run the full 6-model x 6-dataset grid (not just the 3x6 n=120 subset) under >=3 paraphrases, and report how often the band-level verdict flips, since several reported verdicts (e.g., SBIC Promising vs Mixed, HateXplain Mixed vs Caution) sit near band boundaries and the paper already shows only 10/18 cells are exactly stable.

Search-recall validation: assemble a seed set of 20-30 canonical HCI/CSCW/qualitative-methods LLM-substitution papers (independently of the pipeline) and report what fraction the LLM-assisted search arm recovered, to quantify corpus completeness and venue bias.
minor_comments: The model identifiers (deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5.1, qwen3.6, gptoss-120b, gemma-4-31b) and a reference dated 2026 (e.g., [30] arXiv:2603.06865, [39] arXiv:2602.18352) read as forward-dated/fictional or non-verifiable; for double-blind review this is fine if real, but please ensure all model versions and citations are real, archivally stable, and dated correctly, since several appear to postdate plausible submission timelines.

The document is described as a paper but is formatted with a table of contents and 8+ appendices spanning ~30 pages; confirm it fits CHI page/format constraints (this reads closer to a journal/arXiv manuscript than a CHI submission).

Figure quality/legibility: several figures (Figs 3b, 11, 12) have severely garbled axis labels in the extracted text (model names broken across characters); ensure final figures are legible and that the dataset-ordering claim ('ordered by construct subjectivity') is consistent between Fig 11 and Table 4.

The abstract states '36 augment vs 14 replace vs 5 simulate' = 55, but please reconcile with the per-method modal-role table (Table 3) where several 'replace' methods appear; a study-level vs method-level role count distinction should be stated to avoid confusion.

Define 'expertise gap' (judges converge to crowd not expert consensus, ref [4]) earlier and connect it explicitly to the HCC concern that LLM judges may systematically discount expert/minority readings; this is one of the most HCC-relevant findings and is currently buried.

Cohen's kappa is reported as a primary chance-corrected metric, but for the high-base-rate binary tasks (SBIC, MultiPICo) kappa is known to be unstable under prevalence; consider also reporting Gwet's AC1 or prevalence-adjusted kappa.
The 'no human-subjects / IRB exemption' statement is reasonable, but the human IRR study requested above would involve human coders and should be planned with appropriate consent/exemption.

Clarify whether the 'institutional vLLM API' single-provider constraint could induce correlated errors in the 6-model panel/jury (shared serving stack, quantization fp8), which would inflate jury agreement; a brief note on independence assumptions for the jury aggregation would help.

expertise: Statistics, experimental design, and psychometrics (mixed-effects models, inter-rater reliability, FDR, power analysis, bootstrap inference, measurement theory)
summary: The paper synthesizes four studies on when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing: two PRISMA systematic reviews (217 cross-domain and 55 HCC-specific studies), a re-run of the SummEval/G-Eval benchmark with frontier open models (Study 1b), and a fresh meta-evaluation of a 6-model open panel on six HCC datasets with human labels (Study 3, 18,000 judgments), anchored to a human-human agreement ceiling and analyzed with a logistic GLMM, an alt-test, bootstrap CIs, and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control. The central, well-framed claim is that LLM-judge reliability is bounded by the human agreement ceiling and is governed by item-level human (dis)agreement rather than by a clean dataset-level "subjectivity" effect or by model scale. From my statistics/psychometrics lens, the inferential machinery is unusually careful for this literature (ceiling anchoring, leave-one-dataset-out robustness, cluster-robust SEs, FDR, a clean contamination control, prompt-robustness checks, and commendable self-correction of an over-claimed subjectivity coefficient). However, the load-bearing inferential claims rest on only six datasets at Level 2, the reported GLMM is statistically underspecified and partly incoherent (no model-level random effect despite a reported between-model SD, six dataset clusters for "robust" SEs, missing fit/convergence/priors), the human-ceiling definitions are not psychometrically uniform across radically different annotator counts (~100 vs ~3), the power analysis is mis-specified for the actual one-sided below-ceiling test, and the alt-test pass/fail is reported without uncertainty. These are fixable but currently undermine the strength of several causal-sounding claims.
strengths: Ceiling-anchoring as the core methodological commitment is the right move and is executed seriously: reliability is benchmarked against random-annotator-vs-majority accuracy with annotator-bootstrap CIs (Table 5), not a manufactured single gold. This is exactly the psychometrically defensible framing the LLM-judge literature usually lacks.

Admirable statistical self-correction: the authors explicitly retract a naive 'subjectivity' coefficient once they add granularity and base-rate skew, switch to item-cluster-robust SEs, report the dataset-level meta-regression slope (-0.39) as NOT robust to leave-one-dataset-out ([-0.57, -0.01]), and temper the claim. This is the opposite of the usual overclaiming and is rare to see.

The variance-decomposition argument (item random-intercept SD = 2.02 vs dataset 0.73 vs between-model 0.086) is a genuinely useful reframing: it localizes the binding constraint at the item level, and the disagreement-stratified accuracy (acc-low-H vs acc-high-H in Table 5) provides convergent, non-modelling evidence for the same conclusion.

Use of the alt-test (Calderon et al.) to gate 'replacement' claims, plus FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg) across the 36 judge x dataset ceiling-gap tests, plus chance-corrected metrics (Cohen's kappa, macro-F1) alongside accuracy, shows multi-pronged inferential triangulation rather than reliance on one number.

The clean contamination control (verbatim-recall probe on guaranteed-post-cutoff text yielding 0.45 MEDIUM) is exemplary skeptical practice: the authors discover their own diagnostic false-positives and accordingly demote it, rather than reporting MEDIUM tiers as evidence of memorization.

Reproducibility is strong: temperature 0, documented model identifiers, idempotent resumable harness, auto-generated statistics macros, released corpora and code, zero API errors and 100% parse rate reported.
Verified internal consistency: the n=500 CI half-width (+/-4.4%) and the >80% power claim for a 0.10 gap are arithmetically correct under the stated assumptions.
weaknesses: The GLMM as described is underspecified and partially incoherent. Appendix C/§6.3 report random intercepts for dataset and item but the between-model SD (0.086) is computed separately and model scale enters only as a fixed effect; there is no model random effect, so the variance-component comparison (item 2.02 vs dataset 0.73 vs model 0.086) compares quantities estimated under different specifications and on different scales (logit random-intercept SDs vs a raw between-model accuracy SD). This is not an apples-to-apples decomposition and cannot support the headline 'item variance dominates' claim as stated.

Six datasets is too few for the dataset-level inferences the paper draws. Item-cluster-robust SEs with only 6 clusters at the dataset grouping, a dataset-level meta-regression with n=6, and an FDR procedure whose ceiling estimates derive from 6 datasets are all operating in a regime where cluster-robust asymptotics and meta-regression are unreliable. The authors acknowledge fragility for the subjectivity term but still report base-rate skew (OR=8.2), scale (OR=1.30), and granularity (OR=0.84) as 'robust' from the same six-dataset design; these estimates inherit the same instability and their stated p-values are not credible at this cluster count.

The human-ceiling construct is not psychometrically commensurable across datasets. ChaosNLI has ~100 annotators/item while SBIC/HateXplain have ~3. Random-annotator-vs-majority accuracy is a biased and high-variance estimator of the 'ceiling' when A=3 (the majority is defined by 2 of 3 annotators, so the held-out-vs-majority quantity is mechanically inflated/distorted), and it is a very different statistic than with A=100. Comparing judge-vs-ceiling gaps across these regimes without a finite-annotator correction confounds annotator count with subjectivity, which is precisely the confound the paper claims to control.

The power analysis is specified for the wrong test. The design's substantive test is a one-sided 'is the judge below the ceiling' comparison where the ceiling is itself estimated with uncertainty (Table 5 CIs), not a fixed constant. The stated '>80% power for a 0.10 gap' treats the ceiling as known and ignores the additional variance from estimating it (especially with A=3 annotators), so the true power for the below-ceiling claims is overstated and unquantified.

The alt-test results (e.g., SBIC 0/6, MultiPICo 5/6) are reported as bare pass/fail counts with no CI or significance on the winning rate itself. The per-cell winning rates in Figure 11 hover at 0.42-0.55, i.e., right at the 0.5 decision boundary; without uncertainty on WR, the binary 'pass/fail' verdicts (and hence the 'no model earns the right to replace a human' claim) are likely not statistically distinguishable from chance for several cells.

The reliability verdicts (the dependent variable of both reviews and a key construct) are produced by an LLM-assisted pipeline and validated only against LLM-assisted re-coders. The reported Krippendorff alpha = 0.87 therefore measures rubric self-consistency between correlated automated coders, not inter-rater reliability in any human-measurement sense. The paper flags this, but it is a fundamental measurement-validity limitation: there is no human IRR anchor for the entire review-based half of the contribution, and the alpha is not evidence of construct validity.

Study 1b's central comparison is statistically asymmetric and underpowered as a claim: four open models' freshly computed summary-level Spearman values are compared to a single set of REPORTED G-Eval numbers with no CI on either side and no test. '3 of 4 beat the reported average' is a descriptive statement dressed as a finding; with summary-level Spearman averaged over 100 documents the sampling variability is large and untested, so 'match or exceed' is not statistically substantiated.

Many Study 1a domain means rest on tiny n (several n<=3, e.g., MT n=3, Dialogue n=2, Argument Quality n=1) yet are assigned green/amber/red traffic lights and plotted as point estimates with apparent precision (Table 1, Figure 3). Averaging an ordinal 1-5 verdict across incommensurable metric families (kappa, alpha, Spearman, accuracy, ICC) is itself a questionable measurement operation; reporting a mean of an ordinal verdict to two decimals overstates precision.
required_revisions: Fully specify and correct the GLMM. Report the exact model formula, the random-effects structure (and justify the absence of a model-level random effect or add one), estimation method, priors (it is called Bayesian), convergence diagnostics (R-hat/ESS or singular-fit warnings), and an apples-to-apples variance-component table on a single common scale (logit). Re-derive the 'item dominates' claim only from a model where dataset, item, and model random effects are estimated jointly.

Replace or supplement the 6-cluster cluster-robust SEs and n=6 meta-regression with inference appropriate to the small number of dataset clusters (e.g., wild cluster bootstrap, or explicitly recast all dataset-level claims as descriptive). Apply leave-one-dataset-out robustness to ALL reported fixed effects (base-rate skew, scale, granularity), not only to subjectivity, and report the resulting bounds.

Make the human ceiling commensurable. Either restrict ceiling-gap inferences to a finite-annotator-corrected estimator, or report ceilings under a common sub-sample of annotators (e.g., bootstrap A=3 from ChaosNLI to match SBIC/HateXplain) so annotator count is not confounded with subjectivity. State explicitly how the held-out-vs-majority statistic behaves at A=3 and correct for it.

Re-run the power analysis for the actual one-sided below-ceiling test with the ceiling estimated (not fixed), propagating the ceiling's sampling variance; report achieved power per dataset given the realized ceiling CIs.

Attach uncertainty to the alt-test. Report a CI (or permutation/bootstrap p-value) on each judge x dataset winning rate and re-state the pass/fail verdicts as significance against 0.5, flagging cells that are statistically indistinguishable from the boundary.

Obtain at least one genuinely independent HUMAN re-coding of a sample of both review corpora and report human-vs-pipeline agreement, since all current reliability figures come from correlated LLM-assisted coders. If infeasible, downgrade every review-based quantitative claim (means, traffic lights, role/stance percentages) to explicitly qualitative status.

Add tests/CIs to Study 1b: report bootstrap CIs over the 100 documents for each open model's summary-level Spearman and a paired test (or at minimum CI overlap) against the G-Eval reference; remove or soften 'match or exceed' wherever the comparison is not statistically supported.

Stop reporting two-decimal means of an ordinal 1-5 verdict for domains/methods with n<=3, and remove or grey out traffic-light assignments for n=1-2 cells; report these only as raw verdict listings. Justify (or replace) averaging an ordinal verdict across heterogeneous metric families.
requested_experiments: Annotator-count ablation on the ceiling: for ChaosNLI-SNLI/MNLI (A~100), subsample to A=3 and A=5 annotators per item, recompute the random-annotator-vs-majority ceiling and the judge-vs-ceiling gap, and report how the gap and verdict change. This directly tests whether the NLI 'low ceiling / judges win' result is an artifact of annotator count rather than of subjectivity.

Refit the GLMM with crossed random intercepts for item, dataset, and model jointly (and a random slope for scale by dataset if estimable), report the variance components on the logit scale with CIs, and report ICCs. Show the item-vs-dataset-vs-model dominance claim survives this single coherent specification.

Wild cluster bootstrap (datasets as clusters, e.g., 9999 reps) for the fixed-effects p-values of subjectivity, base-rate skew, scale, and granularity; report the bootstrapped CIs alongside the naive cluster-robust ones.

Bootstrap the alt-test winning rate per judge x dataset cell (resampling annotators and items) to produce 95% CIs on WR, and recompute the pass/fail table at the 0.5 threshold with significance flags; report how many of the 36 cells flip or become indeterminate.

Bootstrap CIs and a paired Wilcoxon (or bootstrap difference) test for Study 1b summary-level Spearman per aspect per model over the 100 source documents against the G-Eval reference, to quantify whether 'beats reported G-Eval' is significant.

Human IRR study: have 2 independent human coders (not LLM-assisted) re-code a random ~25% sample of each corpus on the verdict, role, and stance codes; report Krippendorff alpha and compare to the LLM-assisted alpha to bound the rubric's construct validity.

Sensitivity of the FDR ceiling-gap analysis to the ceiling estimator: rerun the Benjamini-Hochberg analysis using (a) pairwise human-human agreement and (b) the finite-annotator-corrected ceiling, and report whether the count of cells significantly below ceiling (currently 22/26) is stable.

Add 2-3 additional subjective HCC datasets with >5 annotators/item (e.g., MD-Agreement/Brexit stance, or a multi-annotator toxicity set) to move the dataset-level inferences out of the n=6 regime, and re-estimate the meta-regression.
minor_comments: Table 4 lists the SBIC human ceiling as 0.91 while the abstract/text say 0.905 and Table 5 gives [0.89,0.92]; harmonize the reported ceiling values and decimal precision throughout.

Figure 13a reports the subjectivity OR as 0.17 (p=1e-07) in the naive model, but the text reports OR=0.74 (p=0.45) in the adjusted model. Make explicit in the figure caption that panel (a) is the discredited naive model, to avoid readers citing 0.17.

The between-model accuracy SD (0.086) is on the probability scale while item/dataset SDs (2.02, 0.73) are logit random-intercept SDs; never present these three numbers in the same sentence/figure without converting to a common scale.
State the exact number of annotators per item used for each dataset (Table 6 gives ranges like '~3-5' and '3-8'); the ceiling estimator and its variance depend critically on the exact A distribution.

Clarify how 'ties dropped' in sampling to n=500 affects the base-rate skew predictor and the ceiling, since dropping ties removes the most ambiguous (high-disagreement) items and could bias the ceiling upward.
Report whether the 1000-resample bootstrap resamples items only or items-and-annotators; for ceiling CIs the annotator resampling is essential and should be stated.
The alt-test winning-rate decision threshold of exactly 0.5 is treated as crisp; note that Calderon et al.'s test has its own assumptions (epsilon/cost parameters) and state the configuration used.
Define 'granularity' (number of classes) numerically per dataset and confirm it is not perfectly collinear with subjectivity given only 6 datasets, otherwise the OR=0.84 is uninterpretable.

area: Responsible AI, data contamination, and ML generalization
expertise: Responsible AI, data contamination, ML generalization, and LLM evaluation methodology (LLM-as-a-judge, annotator-disagreement / perspectivist NLP).
summary: The paper bundles four studies into a single account of when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing (HCC). Study 1a is a PRISMA review of 217 cross-domain studies producing a green/amber/red reliability landscape anchored to the human-human ceiling (mean reliability 3.15/5). Study 1b re-runs the G-Eval/SummEval summary-level protocol with four frontier open models to adjudicate "matter of time" vs "structural ceiling," finding open models now match/exceed reported GPT-4 correlations on verifiable aspects (consistency, coherence) but not on the compressed fluency aspect. Study 2 is a PRISMA review of 55 HCC/HCI/qualitative studies, coding method x role x stance and finding an augment-not-replace consensus under a dominant epistemic stance of tension. Study 3 is the empirical core: a 6-model open panel (9B-754B) judging 6 datasets (ChaosNLI-SNLI/MNLI, GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain, MultiPICo) at n=500 each (18000 judgments), benchmarked against the human ceiling with an alt-test, a jury, a verbatim contamination probe, and a mixed-effects analysis. The headline claim is a single organizing principle: an LLM judge can only be reliable to the degree the human task has a high, verifiable agreement ceiling, and scaling does not raise that ceiling; the binding constraint is item-level human disagreement, not contamination or model scale. The paper is unusually self-critical about its own limitations (contamination probe invalidity, non-robust subjectivity coefficient, LLM-assisted re-coding) and offers a practitioner decision framework. Through my assigned lens, the central empirical mechanism (item-level disagreement governs reliability) is plausible and reasonably supported, but the contamination story is methodologically broken (a probe the authors themselves declare invalid still props up the "rules out memorization" claim), generalization rests on a single provider and a tiny n=6 dataset base, and the structural-vs-temporal distinction is asserted more strongly than the evidence licenses.
strengths: Timely, well-motivated question for CHI: the augment/replace/simulate framing and the human-ceiling-anchoring commitment are genuinely useful contributions to how HCC should govern LLM-as-a-judge, and the decision framework is actionable.

The core empirical finding of Study 3 is the most defensible part: judge correctness tracks item-level human (dis)agreement (random-intercept SD_item = 2.02 >> dataset 0.73 and model 0.086), judges lose accuracy on high-entropy items, and chance-corrected metrics (Cohen's kappa, macro-F1) confirm the gap is not a base-rate artifact. The mixed-effects design with item-cluster-robust SEs and BH correction across 36 cells is appropriate.

Commendable scientific honesty: the authors retract their own naive subjectivity coefficient once granularity and base-rate skew are added (OR=0.74, p=0.45), report a leave-one-dataset-out interval that crosses zero, and explicitly downgrade the contamination probe after a clean-control false-positive. This self-correction is rare and raises trust in the surviving claims.

Strong reproducibility posture: released corpora (217 + 55), search logs, harnesses, temperature-0 idempotent runs, auto-generated stat macros, power justification (n=500, CI half-width <=4.4%), zero API errors / 100% parse rate, and a thoughtful release policy (item-level labels only, no raw sensitive text).

Good engagement with perspectivist / learning-with-disagreement literature (Plank, Aroyo & Welty, ChaosNLI/MultiPICo), and the use of the alt-test [9] to gate replacement claims is the right methodological move that most LLM-judge papers omit.

Ethics handling of HateXplain/SBIC is careful: processing only for moderation reliability, never redistributing raw harmful text, and an explicit reflexive acknowledgement that treating the majority label as ground truth itself encodes majority-group bias.
weaknesses: Contamination claim is self-undermining and overstated. The paper's own clean control shows the verbatim-recall probe false-positives at 0.45 (MEDIUM) on guaranteed-unseen text, so the authors declare it 'not a reliable leakage test' and that uniform MEDIUM tiers are 'largely a probe artifact.' Yet the abstract, Study 3 results, and Section 6.4 still assert that the MultiPICo result 'rules out contamination as the explanation' and provides 'an independent contamination control.' You cannot rule out contamination with an instrument you have just shown is invalid. The single surviving signal (one low-recall model scoring LOW on MultiPICo) is a single-model, single-dataset anecdote, not a contamination control. The argument also conflates 'not memorized verbatim' with 'not contaminated' - exposure can inflate classification accuracy without verbatim recall (n-gram overlap, paraphrase, label leakage via downstream task data). The structural-vs-memorization adjudication therefore rests on a broken probe.

Generalization is severely limited and the paper's framing oversells it. Study 3 uses open models from a SINGLE institutional provider/serving stack (vLLM), one prompt family, and only 6 datasets - five of which are pre-2024 public benchmarks almost certainly in pretraining. No proprietary frontier judge (GPT/Claude/Gemini) is tested in Study 3, despite Study 1b explicitly motivating the comparison. The 'scale does not raise the ceiling' claim is drawn from a model size range and family set that is not designed to isolate scale (gemma-4-31b ties glm-5.1 754B - this could be training-data/instruction-tuning differences, not a ceiling). Provider monoculture is a real external-validity threat for any Responsible-AI claim about LLM judges 'in general.'

The structural vs temporal distinction is the paper's marquee conceptual contribution but is under-identified. Study 1b infers a 'structural ceiling' on fluency partly from the fact that a noiseless judge returning rounded human means would attain rho ~ 0.87 - which actually shows the residual gap is largely reference compression, i.e. an artifact of integer scoring and low rating variance, NOT a structural property of judging. The authors hedge this in 4.2 but then carry the strong 'structural ceiling' framing into the abstract and discussion. The distinction is asserted across only ONE benchmark (SummEval) for the temporal arm and is not orthogonally tested (no probability-weighted scoring run, which they admit could narrow the gap).

Mixing of incommensurable evidence and verdict rubric subjectivity. The five-level reliability verdict is an ordinal judgement applied across heterogeneous metrics (kappa, alpha, Spearman, accuracy, ICC, F1) and pooled into domain means (e.g. Table 1 averages a kappa-based study and a Spearman-based study into '3.15/5'). The reported alpha=0.87 reproducibility is between LLM-assisted re-coders applying the same rubric to the same summaries - this measures prompt/rubric determinism, not validity, and cannot rule out a shared systematic bias in how the LLM pipeline reads studies. With no human IRR, the entire review layer is an LLM judging the LLM-judge literature, which is exactly the methodological monoculture the paper warns about.

Internal inconsistency / possible data error in the headline tables undermines trust. The SBIC human ceiling is reported as 0.905 in the abstract/Section 6.2 and Table 6, as 0.91 in Table 4, and best-model 0.86 (glm) called the 'strongest social-computing case (Promising)'; but Figure 12 shows glm SBIC at 0.86/Prom while the per-aspect text and alt-test (0/6) say no model can replace a human there. The narrative that SBIC is simultaneously 'Promising' and 'fails the alt-test 0/6' needs reconciling - a Promising verdict that fails the replacement test for every model is confusing and the rubric does not clearly separate 'agreement-near-ceiling' from 'safe-to-replace.'

Over-generalization from annotation to the full HCC decision framework. The 'Keep humans' regime (participant simulation, personas, expert design judgment) is the most consequential claim for CHI, yet it is supported ONLY by the Study 2 review, not by any Study 3 experiment (the authors concede this in Scope of Evidence). The paper's title and abstract imply an empirically grounded verdict across HCC methods, but the experimental evidence covers only subjective text classification. This gap between claim scope and evidence scope is a soundness issue at CHI.
required_revisions: Fix the contamination claim end-to-end. Either (a) remove all language asserting that contamination is 'ruled out' / that there is an 'independent contamination control' (abstract, Section 6.2, 6.4, 7.3), and restate the claim as 'the disagreement analysis, not contamination control, supports our interpretation; contamination cannot be excluded with our probe'; or (b) add a valid contamination diagnostic (see requested experiments) and re-derive the claim. The current text simultaneously calls the probe invalid and uses it as evidence - this must be resolved.

Add at least one proprietary frontier judge (e.g. GPT-4-class, Claude-class, Gemini-class) to Study 3, or explicitly and prominently scope every Study 3 conclusion to 'open models served via a single vLLM provider' in the abstract, contributions, and conclusion - not only in Limitations. The provider/family monoculture must not be buried.

Disentangle scale from family/training in the 'scale does not raise the ceiling' claim. Either run a within-family scale ladder (e.g. qwen3.x at 9B/27B/larger holding family fixed) to identify scale, or downgrade the claim to 'within our heterogeneous panel, larger size did not monotonically improve subjective-task accuracy,' and remove the implication that this is a property of scale per se.

Soften and properly bound the 'structural ceiling' framing in Study 1b. State clearly in the abstract and discussion that the fluency shortfall is substantially a reference-compression/integer-scoring artifact (rho~0.87 noiseless bound) and run the probability-weighted scoring you cite as a remedy before claiming any aspect is structurally unbeatable. One benchmark cannot establish a general structural-vs-temporal taxonomy; either add a second promising-band benchmark or reframe as a case study.

Reconcile the SBIC 'Promising but alt-test 0/6' inconsistency and standardize the ceiling values (0.905 vs 0.91) across abstract, Table 4, Table 6, and figures. Clarify in the rubric the relationship between the five-level verdict and the alt-test, since a 'Promising' verdict that fails replacement for all models is currently contradictory.

Add genuine human inter-rater reliability on a sample of both review corpora (at least the verdict and role codes), since the alpha=0.87/0.85 figures are LLM-vs-LLM rubric determinism and cannot validate the coding against the monoculture critique the paper itself raises. Report human-LLM agreement, not only LLM-LLM.

Align claim scope with evidence scope: revise the title/abstract/contributions so they do not imply experimental grounding for participant simulation, personas, and design judgment. Make explicit (not only in 'Scope of evidence') that the 'Keep humans' regime is review-grounded, and that Study 3 tests subjective text classification only.
requested_experiments: Valid contamination diagnostics replacing the broken verbatim probe: (1) the 'guided vs general' instruction probe (Golchin & Surdeanu) and/or membership-inference via min-k% token probability on each Study-3 dataset; (2) for any dataset with a temporal split, a time-stratified accuracy comparison (pre- vs post-cutoff items); (3) a label-perturbation test - shuffle gold labels and measure whether the judge still 'recalls' the original, indicating memorized label associations. Report whether subjective-task accuracy correlates with any memorization signal.

Add 2-3 proprietary frontier judges (GPT-4-class, Claude-class, Gemini-class) on all 6 Study-3 datasets at n=500, reporting accuracy, kappa, macro-F1, alt-test winning rate, and acc-low-disagreement vs acc-high-disagreement, to test whether the 'ceiling governs' and 'scale does not help' findings hold across providers, not just one vLLM stack.

Within-family scale ladder: run qwen3.x at >=3 sizes (e.g. 9B, 27B, and the largest available) holding the family and prompt fixed, on the three high-ceiling subjective datasets (GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain), and fit accuracy vs log-params within family to isolate a scale effect from training-data/family confounds.

Run probability-weighted (token-logprob expected-value) scoring for Study 1b SummEval fluency, as the authors say could narrow the gap, and report whether the 'structural' fluency shortfall persists; add a second promising-band benchmark (e.g. a RAG-faithfulness or dialogue dataset with expert human ratings) and repeat the open-vs-reported comparison to test the structural/temporal taxonomy beyond one case.

Soft-label / distributional evaluation: report judge-vs-human cross-entropy or KL against the full human label distribution (not only majority accuracy) for all 6 datasets, and a per-subgroup disagreement analysis on every dataset that carries annotator IDs (not only MultiPICo), to substantiate the epistemic-injustice / minority-flattening claim with more than one corpus.

Prompt-robustness at full scale: extend the n=120 paraphrase robustness check to the full panel x all 6 datasets at n=500 with >=3 paraphrases, and report verdict stability (exact and within-one-level) per cell, since 8/18 cells already flip a level in the pilot and several headline verdicts (e.g. SBIC Promising, HateXplain Mixed) sit on band boundaries.
minor_comments: Reference [30] (James et al., 2026, arXiv:2603.06865) and [39] (arXiv:2602.18352), [38]/others cite arXiv IDs with month '26' (2026) - verify these are real and not hallucinated future-dated preprints; future-dated arXiv IDs are a red flag for a contamination/Responsible-AI reviewer.

Model identifiers (deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5.1, qwen3.6-35b-a3b, gemma-4-31b) are non-standard/future-sounding; pin exact released checkpoints, quantization (fp8 is mentioned for some), and serving versions in a table so the run is reproducible and the 'frontier open' claim is verifiable.

Study 1b uses integer-only (1-5) scoring; state explicitly that this caps Spearman and interacts with the fluency-compression argument; the noiseless-bound rho=0.87 should appear in the figure caption, not only the body.

The 'mean reliability 3.15/5' headline averages ordinal verdicts across incommensurable metrics and small-n domains (several n<=3); avoid reporting a single decimal mean as if it were an interval-scale quantity, or report a distribution instead.

Figure 11/12 are partly garbled in the manuscript text extraction (overlapping labels, WR values interleaved); ensure final typeset figures are legible - several axis labels and model names are scrambled.

Clarify how 'ties dropped' in dataset sampling (n=500 with clear majority) affects the difficulty distribution - dropping ties removes the hardest, highest-disagreement items and could bias the ceiling and the disagreement analysis upward.

The alt-test winning-rate threshold of >=0.5 is used as the replacement bar; justify this threshold (vs the original paper's recommendation) and report the test's statistical error rate at n~3 annotators/item for SBIC/HateXplain, where leave-one-annotator-out is very low-powered.
